1: “Then Jesus told his disciples a parable to show them that they should always pray and not give up (enkakein, lose heart)” — Luke’s characteristic paraenetic framing states the parable’s purpose before telling it. The persistent prayer-instruction is grounded in the eschatological context of Luke 17: the disciples who are waiting for the Son of Man’s coming need a theology of prayer that sustains hope across the interval of waiting. The command “always pray and not give up” anticipates Paul’s “pray continually” (1 Thessalonians 5:17) and the promised Spirit-intercession (Romans 8:26–27) that sustains prayer when it feels futile.
2: “In a certain city there was a judge who neither feared God nor cared what people thought” — the unjust judge’s double impiety (no fear of God, no shame before people) is the exact inversion of the biblical ideal: “fear God and keep his commandments” (Ecclesiastes 12:13); “do justice, love mercy, and walk humbly with your God” (Micah 6:8). The parable works by contrast (not comparison) with God: if even this godless judge eventually grants justice, how much more will the God who is utterly faithful to his own character bring justice to his chosen ones?
3: “And there was a widow in that city who kept coming to him with the plea: Grant me justice against my opponent” — the widow as the paradigmatic vulnerable person in the OT justice system (Exodus 22:22–23: “do not take advantage of a widow or an orphan. If you do and they cry out to me, I will certainly hear their cry”; Isaiah 1:17: “defend the cause of the fatherless, plead the case of the widow”). The widow’s persistence is the model for the disciples’ prayer — her cry for justice mirrors the church’s cry (“Maranatha” = “Come, Lord”; Revelation 22:20) for the eschatological justice of the Son of Man’s coming.
4: “For some time he refused. But finally he said to himself: Even though I don’t fear God or care about people…” — the judge’s self-disclosure reveals the absence of any proper motivation for justice: no fear of God, no concern for reputation. His eventual compliance is purely self-interested — he wants to be left alone. The contrast with God heightens the parable’s logic: God has every motivation for justice (his own character, his covenant love for his people, his commitment to his own name), whereas the judge has none — and yet even the unjust judge grants the petition.
5: “Yet because this widow keeps bothering me, I will see that she gets justice, so that she won’t eventually come and attack me!” — the judge’s comic self-interest in avoiding harassment is the entirely unworthy motivation that still produces justice. The humor of the parable is the absurdity of comparing God’s response to this: God is nothing like the judge. The persistence that wore down the judge is the pattern of prayer Christ commends — not because God needs wearing down, but because persistence in prayer is the expression of faith that does not despair of his faithfulness (Hebrews 10:23: “let us hold unswerving to the hope we profess, for he who promised is faithful”).
6: “And the Lord said: Listen to what the unjust judge says” — the instruction to attend carefully to the unjust judge’s words is the rhetorical device of the kal wa-homer (lesser to greater) argument. If the corrupt judge’s words reveal that persistent petition wins justice from the most uncooperative source, the conclusion about God’s justice is irresistible. Luke’s narrative address (“the Lord said”) is one of the rare cases where the narrator uses the title “Lord” (ho Kyrios) for Jesus in narrative — signaling the divine authority of what follows.
7: “And will not God bring about justice for his chosen ones, who cry out to him day and night? Will he keep putting them off?” — the rhetorical question that applies the parable. The “chosen ones” (eklektos, the elect) cry out day and night — the perpetual cry of the suffering church for vindication and justice. The patience of God (“will he keep putting them off?” — expected answer: no) is the context for the promise of swift justice (Luke 18:8). Christologically, the cry of the elect is answered definitively at the cross (where the Judge bears the penalty that justice required) and consummated at the parousia.
8: “I tell you, he will see that they get justice, and quickly. However, when the Son of Man comes, will he find faith on the earth?” — the promise of swift justice is paired with the most searching eschatological question of the travel narrative. The Son of Man’s coming will bring the justice the elect have prayed for — but the question is whether the praying community will still exist: “will he find faith?” The juxtaposition of guaranteed justice and uncertain faith is the call to perseverance. The Son of Man comes as both vindicator and judge — and the parable of the widow is the call to maintain the persistent faith that his coming will reward.
9: “To some who were confident of their own righteousness and looked down on everyone else, Jesus told this parable” — the Pharisee/tax-collector parable is explicitly addressed to those with settled confidence in their own righteousness (pepoithotas eph heautois hoti eisin dikaioi). The target is not Pharisaic hypocrisy per se but the deeper problem of self-justification — the attempt to stand before God on the basis of one’s own moral achievement. The parable will demonstrate that justification comes from God alone, received in humility rather than claimed through performance.
10: Two men go up to the Temple to pray — both are practicing Jews engaged in legitimate religious activity. The Temple as the setting for the parable is significant: the place of atonement-sacrifice (the Yom Kippur rituals of Lev 16) is where both men approach God. The Pharisee’s prayer will reveal his assumption that he needs no atonement; the tax collector’s will reveal his knowledge that he does. The contrast is the contrast between those who approach the Cross as observers and those who approach it as desperate sinners.
11: “The Pharisee stood and prayed about himself: God, I thank you that I am not like other people — robbers, evildoers, adulterers — or even like this tax collector” — the Pharisee’s prayer is genuinely a prayer of thanksgiving — he is not lying about his observance. The problem is the comparison: his righteousness is defined in relation to other people rather than in relation to God’s holiness. The prayer “about himself” (pros heauton) is also possibly “to himself” — the prayer that addresses God in form but the self in content. It is a prayer that never looks up because it is busy looking around.
12: “I fast twice a week and give a tenth of all I get” — the Pharisee’s specific observances (fasting beyond Torah requirements; tithing even garden herbs, Matthew 23:23) are scrupulous and genuine. He is not a hypocrite in the sense of pretending to things he does not do — he really does fast and tithe. The problem is the frame: these observances are the currency with which he believes he has purchased divine approval. The cross exposes the impossibility of this transaction: “a man is not justified by the works of the law” (Galatians 2:16). No amount of fasting and tithing can purchase what only grace can give.
13: “But the tax collector stood at a distance. He would not even look up towards heaven, but beat his breast and said: God, have mercy on me, a sinner” — the posture and prayer of the tax collector are the model of authentic approach to God. The distance from the sanctuary, the downcast eyes, the breast-beating (the sign of grief and self-condemnation in Jewish mourning), and the single plea for mercy (hilasthēti moi, “be propitiated toward me” — the atonement vocabulary of Lev 16) reveal a person who knows he has no merit to offer. His prayer is the prayer that the entire sacrificial system was designed to enable.
14: “I tell you that this man, rather than the other, went home justified before God. For all those who exalt themselves will be humbled, and those who humble themselves will be exalted” — the verdict is Paul’s justification doctrine in narrative form. “Justified” (dedikaiōmenos) is the legal term for acquittal — declared righteous before the divine Judge. The tax collector who offers only the prayer of a sinner goes home with the verdict the Pharisee assumed he had already secured. Christologically, the tax collector’s justification is possible because the cross provides what his prayer could not — actual atonement (hilasmos, 1 John 2:2) before God.
15: “People were bringing babies to Jesus for him to place his hands on them” — the blessing of infants (brephē, newborns) follows immediately after the tax collector’s justification by humble faith. The connection is Christological: those who cannot do anything to merit entry into the kingdom (infants) are the paradigm of kingdom-citizenship (Luke 18:17). The disciples’ rebuke of the parents reflects their misunderstanding of who belongs in the kingdom — the same misunderstanding that the parable of the two prayers exposes in a different register. Jesus corrects both: the self-justifying and the dismissal of the vulnerable.
16: “Let the little children come to me, and do not hinder them, for the kingdom of God belongs to such as these” — the kingdom belongs to the childlike. The children are not praised for innocence (the NT does not idealize childhood as sinless) but for their receptivity — they come to Jesus without pretense of merit and receive his touch. The “such as these” (tōn toioutōn) indicates that children are the model, not the only members: the kingdom belongs to those who approach as children. Christ’s embrace of infants is a type of the new creation’s welcome — “let the one who is thirsty come; let the one who wishes take the free gift of the water of life” (Revelation 22:17).
17: “Truly I tell you, anyone who will not receive the kingdom of God like a little child will never enter it” — the condition for kingdom entry is the childlike posture of receptivity. The child who accepts a gift without earning it is the model for receiving the kingdom — which is itself a gift (Luke 12:32: “your Father has been pleased to give you the kingdom”). The impossibility of earning the kingdom by achievement (the Rich Ruler will demonstrate this in Luke 18:18–27) is the counterpart to the possibility of receiving it as a gift. Christ as the giver of the kingdom demands only the openness of the child’s hands.
18: “A certain ruler asked him: Good teacher, what must I do to inherit eternal life?” — the Rich Ruler’s question contains its own theological problem: “what must I do” assumes that eternal life is achieved through action. The “inherit” (klēronomeō) language is inheritance language — and inheritance is typically received as a gift from a benefactor, not earned. The Christological irony: he asks the one who gives eternal life as a free gift how to earn it. His “doing” frame will be exposed by the radical demand of Luke 18:22 — which he cannot meet — and the resolution of Luke 18:27 (“what is impossible with man is possible with God”).
19: “Why do you call me good? No one is good — except God alone” — Jesus’s response is not a denial of his own goodness but a Christological probe: do you know what you’re saying when you call me good? If goodness belongs only to God, and you are calling me good, you are making a theological claim that requires examination. The question is the Socratic method applied to Christology: Jesus forces the ruler to think through the implications of his address. For those who confess that Jesus is Lord and God (John 20:28: “my Lord and my God!”), calling him “good” is entirely correct — Jesus is not denying this; he is testing whether the ruler knows what he is saying.
20: Jesus’s citation of the commandments (adultery, murder, theft, false testimony, honor parents) from the second table of the Decalogue (Exodus 20:12–16) focuses on the relational commandments — duties to others. The omission of the first table (duties to God, including the prohibition of covetousness) is not accidental — the ruler’s violation will be revealed as covetousness: his attachment to his wealth is the “other gods” before the LORD (Exodus 20:3–4). Christ as the one who fulfills the law (Matthew 5:17) and embodies its summary (“love the Lord your God… love your neighbor”) is the one whose question probes the ruler’s obedience to both tables.
21: “All these I have kept since I was a boy” — the ruler’s self-assessment is sincere, not self-deceptive. Mark’s parallel notes that “Jesus looked at him and loved him” (Mark 10:21) — the response of genuine affection for someone near the kingdom. Yet the claim of complete second-table obedience since boyhood reveals the self-righteousness that the Pharisee’s prayer exposed: confident of one’s own righteousness before God. The first table’s requirement has not been examined — and Luke 18:22 will expose it through the single demand that unmasks the covetous attachment to wealth.
22: “You still lack one thing. Sell everything you have and give to the poor, and you will have treasure in heaven. Then come, follow me” — the radical demand reveals the first-table violation: his wealth is his god. The “one thing” lacking is the love of God with the whole heart — the single most important commandment that the ruler has not examined. The call to sell and follow is not a general command for all disciples (Luke records wealthy followers: Zacchaeus gives half, not all, Luke 19:8) but the precise revelation of this ruler’s specific idolatry. Christ as the one who requires the whole heart (Deuteronomy 6:5) exposes the hidden idol.
23: “When he heard this, he became very sad, because he was very wealthy” — the sadness is the measure of the attachment: the ruler’s wealth holds him more tightly than the kingdom’s invitation. The word “very wealthy” (plousios sphodra) mirrors the “very sad” (perilypos” egeneto sphodra) — the extreme wealth produces extreme sadness. The ruler goes away, unlike the disciples who “left all and followed” (Luke 5:11,28). The theme of wealth as the competitor to kingdom-devotion runs from the Rich Fool (Luke 12:16–21) through Lazarus/Rich Man (Luke 16:19–31) — and this ruler stands at the crossroads between them.
24: “How hard it is for the rich to enter the kingdom of God!” — Jesus’s observation reverses the common theological assumption that wealth is a sign of divine blessing and therefore a path to the kingdom. The OT prosperity theology (Deuteronomy 28:1–14) is not denied but deepened: wealth can become the very obstacle to the kingdom that it might have prepared its possessor to serve. The Christological irony is complete in 2 Corinthians 8:9: the one who makes the kingdom available “became poor” — the rich Jesus of divine glory impoverished himself so that those impoverished by their wealth’s grip might receive the kingdom.
25: “Indeed, it is easier for a camel to go through the eye of a needle than for someone who is rich to enter the kingdom of God” — the hyperbolic impossibility (the largest animal in Levantine experience / the smallest aperture in daily use) states the case as absolute. The intended shock is the shock of complete impossibility: the disciples’ stunned response (Luke 18:26: “who then can be saved?”) shows they heard it correctly. The hyperbole is not a medical impossibility about a small gate (“Needle’s Eye”) but a rhetorical shock-device — the point is that by human means, the rich man’s salvation is impossible.
26: “Those who heard this asked: Who then can be saved?” — the disciples’ question is the right response to the camel/needle impossibility. If the wealthy — those who appear to have every advantage including God’s material blessing — cannot be saved by their own efforts, then no one can. The disciples move from particular case (this rich man) to universal question (who, then?) — and their question opens space for the decisive Christological answer of Luke 18:27. The question is the human recognition of the problem; Christ’s answer is the divine provision of the solution.
27: “What is impossible with man is possible with God” — the Christological resolution of the camel/needle impossibility. Human salvation (including the salvation of the rich from their idolatrous attachment to wealth) is not achievable by human effort — and God makes it possible. The statement echoes Genesis 18:14 (“Is anything too hard for the LORD?”) and Jeremiah 32:17,27 (“nothing is too hard for you”). The incarnation is the specific demonstration of what is impossible with men being accomplished by God: “God was reconciling the world to himself in Christ” (2 Corinthians 5:19). Zacchaeus (Luke 19:1–10) will be the living proof of this verse.
28: “Peter said to him: We have left all we had to follow you” — Peter’s statement invokes the disciples’ actual practice of the demand that the rich ruler could not meet (Luke 5:11: “they pulled their boats up on shore, left everything and followed him”). The contrast is implicit: the disciples did what the ruler would not. Peter’s statement is not self-congratulation but an implicit question about reward — which Jesus’s answer (Luke 18:29–30) addresses. The “we have left all” is the condition of discipleship that the cross makes possible: “whoever wants to be my disciple must deny themselves and take up their cross daily and follow me” (Luke 9:23).
29: “Truly I tell you, no one who has left home or wife or brothers or parents or children for the sake of the kingdom of God” — the list of what is left (home, wife, family) is the sacrifice of the fundamental goods of Jewish society — the very goods the Rich Ruler was unwilling to sacrifice. The “for the sake of the kingdom of God” is the Christological motivation: the disciples leave these goods not for asceticism but because Christ and his kingdom are worth more. The kingdom-exchange economy operates: what is left for the kingdom is returned multiplied in the kingdom community and in the age to come.
30: “Will fail to receive many times as much in this age, and in the age to come eternal life” — the double reward: present and eschatological. The “many times as much in this age” is the new community of brothers and sisters in Christ (Acts 2:44–45: the sharing community); the eternal life in the age to come is the resurrection inheritance. Christologically, the one who promises this reward is the one who himself left all — home (“the Son of Man has nowhere to lay his head”, Luke 9:58), family (Luke 8:21), and ultimately life — and received in return the universal dominion of Philippians 2:9–11. His pattern is the pattern of the promise.
31: “Jesus took the Twelve aside and told them: We are going up to Jerusalem, and everything that is written by the prophets about the Son of Man will be fulfilled” — the third passion prediction (cf. Luke 9:22,44) is the most explicit about Scriptural fulfillment. The “everything written by the prophets” identifies the cross as the fulfillment of the whole prophetic stream — not one or two proof-texts but the entire prophetic witness. The risen Christ’s Emmaus teaching (Luke 24:27,44–46) will unpack what “everything written by the prophets” means: Moses, all the Prophets, and the Psalms all speak of the Messiah’s suffering and glory.
32: “He will be handed over to the Gentiles. They will mock him, insult him and spit on him” — the passion prediction’s detail is remarkable: handed over to Gentiles, mocked, insulted, spat upon. Each element will be literally fulfilled: handed to Pilate (Luke 23:1), mocked by Herod’s soldiers (Luke 23:11) and the Roman soldiers (Luke 23:36), insulted at the cross (Luke 23:35–39). The spitting recalls the Servant of Isaiah 50:6 (“I did not hide my face from mocking and spitting”). The deliberateness of the prediction — including the specific Gentile and national-shame elements — demonstrates that the passion is not defeat but the execution of the divine plan.
33: “They will flog him and kill him. On the third day he will rise again” — the flogging fulfills Isaiah 53:5 (“by his wounds we are healed”; the Hebrew chaburah/makkah means a lashing wound). The death and third-day resurrection is the central pattern of the new covenant’s inauguration (Hosea 6:2: “after two days he will revive us; on the third day he will restore us”; Jonah’s three days: Matthew 12:40). The precision of the prediction — the third day — transforms the passion from random tragedy into the divinely ordained act by which the new covenant is ratified and the resurrection secured.
34: “The disciples did not understand any of this. Its meaning was hidden from them, and they did not know what he was talking about” — the triple statement of incomprehension (did not understand, hidden, did not know) emphasizes the complete failure of the disciples to receive the passion prediction. The hiddenness is passive divine agency — God conceals for the time being. Luke’s emphasis is not on the disciples’ stupidity but on the eschatological revelation required for understanding: the cross and resurrection must happen before the Scriptures’ meaning becomes clear (Luke 24:45: “he opened their minds so they could understand the Scriptures”).
35: “As Jesus approached Jericho, a blind man was sitting by the roadside begging” — the blind man at Jericho is placed immediately after the disciples’ blindness (Luke 18:34: they cannot see the meaning of the passion prediction). The structural irony is Luke’s commentary: the physically blind man will receive sight; the spiritually blinded disciples will not receive it until the resurrection. Jericho is the city of Joshua’s first conquest (Josh 6) — the entry into the Promised Land. Jesus approaching Jericho on his way to Jerusalem is the approach of the greater Joshua (Yēshua = Jesus) to his final conquest, the cross.
36: “When he heard the crowd going by, he asked what was happening” — the blind man’s initial dependence on hearing mirrors the situation of those who have not yet seen Christ but are informed about him through testimony. The crowd’s witness (Luke 18:37: “Jesus of Nazareth is passing by”) is the testimony that enables faith. The Christological principle: faith comes from hearing (Romans 10:17: “faith comes from hearing the message, and the message is heard through the word about Christ”). The blind man’s hearing creates the opportunity for the sight-giving encounter.
37: “They told him: Jesus of Nazareth is passing by” — the identification of Jesus as “of Nazareth” is the historical, human identification — the one from the obscure Galilean village. The blind man who receives this report responds with a Christological title that exceeds the crowd’s identification (Luke 18:38: “Son of David”) — demonstrating that his spiritual sight, even in physical blindness, surpasses the crowd’s. The contrast between the crowd’s “Jesus of Nazareth” and the blind man’s “Son of David” is the contrast between mere historical awareness and confessional faith.
38: “He called out: Jesus, Son of David, have mercy on me!” — “Son of David” is the messianic title that identifies Jesus as the heir of the Davidic covenant (2 Samuel 7:12–16; Isaiah 11:1–10). The blind man’s use of the title is a confession of faith in Jesus as the Messiah — and the Messiah is the one who restores sight to the blind (Isaiah 35:5: “the eyes of the blind will be opened”; Luke 42:7: “to open eyes that are blind”). His cry for mercy (eleēson) repeats the Kyrie Eleison of the ten lepers (Luke 17:13) — the prayer that reaches the heart of Christ.
39: “Those who led the way rebuked him and told him to be quiet, but he shouted all the more: Son of David, have mercy on me!” — the persistence of the blind man through opposition is the parable of the persistent widow (Luke 18:1–8) enacted in narrative. The crowd’s rebuke parallels the disciples’ rebuke of those bringing infants (Luke 18:15) — in both cases Jesus overrides the disciples to welcome the apparently marginalized. The blind man’s louder cry under pressure demonstrates the faith that the parable of the widow called for: do not give up (Luke 18:1). Persistence before Christ is honored.
40: “Jesus stopped and ordered the man to be brought to him. When he came near, Jesus asked him…” — the stopping of Jesus at the cry of a single blind beggar in a crowd on the road to Jerusalem (where the cross awaits) reveals the consistent Christological priority: individual need commands the full attention of the one on the universal mission. The pattern repeats throughout Luke: Jairus’s daughter and the hemorrhaging woman interrupt one another; Zacchaeus stops the procession. The Son of Man who “came to seek and save the lost” (Luke 19:10) stops for each one individually.
41: “What do you want me to do for you? Lord, I want to see (anablepsō)” — Jesus’s question is the question of the patient, hearing Savior — “what do you want me to do for you?” is also asked of James and John (Mark 10:36), but there the answer is self-serving ambition; here it is the genuine need of the blind. The specific request (hina anablepsō) is fulfilled in Luke 18:43. The blind man’s answer is the model prayer: specific, concrete, addressed to the Lord’s capacity and compassion. He does not say “if you want” — he trusts that Christ both can and will.
42: “Jesus said to him: Receive your sight; your faith has healed you (sesōken)” — the salvation-word again (cf. Luke 7:50; Luke 8:48; Luke 17:19). The healing-salvation formula is the consistent Christological signature: the physical restoration is simultaneously a sign of the eschatological salvation that Christ gives. “Son of David” faith produces “saved” healing — the blind man’s Davidic-messianic confession is the faith that receives the Servant’s gift of sight (Isaiah 35:5; Luke 42:7). The command “receive your sight” (anablepson) is the word alone — no touch, no action, only the Son of David’s sovereign speech.
43: “Immediately he received his sight and followed Jesus, praising God” — the healing produces discipleship and doxology. “Followed Jesus” (ēkolouthei autō) on the road to Jerusalem is following the one who goes to the cross — the newly sighted man joins the passion-procession. His praise of God echoes throughout Luke’s healings (Luke 5:25–26; Luke 7:16; Luke 13:13; Luke 17:15–16) and anticipates the crowd’s praise at the Triumphal Entry (Luke 19:37–38). The people’s echo of his praise is the spreading of the doxology — the community drawn into worship by the sight-giving of the Son of David who is himself the light of the world (John 9:5).
